enonce.texte:
Read the text below carefully and answer the questions that follow.

The Choice to Leave

In recent years, emigration has become a significant phenomenon in many African countries, including Cameroon. Thousands of young professionals, students, and workers choose to leave their home countries in search of better opportunities abroad. The reasons for this decision are complex and varied. Some emigrate to pursue higher education that is not available at home, while others seek better employment prospects and higher salaries. Political instability and limited access to quality healthcare are also factors that push people to leave. However, emigration comes with challenges. Many emigrants struggle with cultural shock, language barriers, and feelings of isolation. They often experience a sense of loss of identity and connection to their homeland. Despite these difficulties, the desire for a better future often outweighs the risks. Some emigrants send remittances back home, contributing to their families' well-being and their country's economy. In many cases, emigration is not a permanent choice; some people return home after gaining experience and skills abroad, while others choose to settle permanently in their new country.
images: A young African professional with a suitcase, standing at an airport departure gate, looking back with a thoughtful expression, symbolizing the emigration journey.

questions.texte: Identify the main topic discussed in the text.
questions.reponse: The main topic is emigration and the various reasons and challenges associated with it.

questions.texte: List three reasons why people emigrate according to the text.
questions.reponse: Three reasons are: to pursue higher education, to seek better employment prospects and higher salaries, and due to political instability.

questions.texte: Explain the challenges faced by emigrants as described in the text.
questions.reponse: The challenges include cultural shock, language barriers, feelings of isolation, and a sense of loss of identity and connection to their homeland.

questions.texte: Analyze the positive contribution of emigrants to their home countries mentioned in the text.
questions.reponse: Emigrants contribute to their home countries by sending remittances, which support their families' well-being and contribute to the country's economy.

questions.texte: Evaluate the text's overall message about emigration. Do you think the text presents a balanced view? Justify your answer.
questions.reponse: The text presents a balanced view. It discusses both the positive reasons for emigration (better opportunities, education, contributions to the home economy) and the negative aspects (challenges, loss of identity, isolation). This balanced perspective allows the reader to understand the complexities of the decision to emigrate.

intitule: Vocabulary in Context: Emigration and Identity

enonce.texte: Match the vocabulary words from the text with their correct definitions.

questions.texte: Match the following words with their definitions.

sousQuestions.reponse: 3. To leave one's own country to settle permanently in another.
5. In or to a foreign country.
1. A set of circumstances that makes it possible to do something.
4. The place where one lives permanently, especially as a member of a family or household.
2. The fact of being who or what a person or thing is.

questions.texte: Use the phrase 'sense of loss' in a new sentence of your own.
questions.reponse: After moving to a new country, many immigrants experience a sense of loss for their familiar culture and traditions. (Accept any grammatically correct sentence that demonstrates understanding of the phrase.)

enonce.texte: Complete the following sentences with the appropriate modal of speculation: may, might, or could.

questions.texte: I'm not sure, but he _____ be planning to emigrate to Canada.
questions.reponse: I'm not sure, but he may/might/could be planning to emigrate to Canada. (All three are possible, as they express possibility.)

questions.texte: She _____ return to Cameroon after her studies, but she hasn't decided yet.
questions.reponse: She may/might/could return to Cameroon after her studies, but she hasn't decided yet. (All three are possible, as they express possibility.)

questions.texte: He is working hard. He _____ be preparing for his visa interview.
questions.reponse: He is working hard. He may/might/could be preparing for his visa interview. (All three are possible, as they express speculation about a current action.)

questions.texte: She hasn't called in a while. She _____ have already left for Europe.
questions.reponse: She hasn't called in a while. She may/might/could have already left for Europe. (This is speculation about a past action, using 'may/might/could + have + past participle'.)

questions.texte: We _____ see a significant increase in emigration if economic conditions don't improve.
questions.reponse: We may/might/could see a significant increase in emigration if economic conditions don't improve. (All three are possible, as they express a future possibility.)

enonce.texte:
You are a young Cameroonian professional who has been offered a job opportunity abroad. Your family is divided: some members encourage you to go, while others want you to stay and contribute to Cameroon. Your task is to write a persuasive letter (250-300 words) to your parents explaining your decision. Your letter must:

  • Explain the reasons for your decision to emigrate or to stay.
  • Discuss the positive and negative aspects of your choice.
  • Address the concept of 'home' and how you perceive your identity.
  • Use at least three modals of speculation (may, might, could) to discuss possibilities.
  • Use appropriate vocabulary related to emigration and identity.
images: A young adult sitting at a desk writing a letter, with a photo of their family on the desk and a globe or world map in the background.

questions.texte: Write your persuasive letter below. Be clear, respectful, and persuasive.
questions.reponse:
**Exemple de réponse (non-exhaustif):**

Dear Mom and Dad,

I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to you today to discuss the job offer I received from the company in France. I know you have mixed feelings about this opportunity, and I want to share my thoughts and feelings with you.

First, I want you to understand why I am seriously considering this opportunity. The job offers not only a much higher salary but also the chance to work with cutting-edge technology that is not yet available in Cameroon. This experience could give me the skills and knowledge I need to make a real difference when I return home. However, I understand your concerns. I might feel isolated in a new country, and I may experience cultural shock. But I believe that these challenges will help me grow as a person.

I know you worry about me being so far away from home. But I want you to know that no matter where I am, Cameroon will always be my home. My identity is deeply rooted in our culture and our values. Going abroad does not mean I am leaving my identity behind; it means I am carrying it with me. I might return after a few years, or I could decide to stay longer, but my heart will always be with you and with Cameroon.

I promise to stay in touch regularly. I also believe that this experience could ultimately benefit not just me, but our entire family and community. I may be able to create networks and bring back knowledge that could help us all.

I hope you will support my decision. I love you both very much.

With all my love,
[Your Name]
